speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 28, 1995. The survivor is Regina Gaska. We're in Forest Hills, New York, and we're speaking in English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I am Sherry Amatenstein. I'll be conducting the interview today. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Could you tell me your name, please, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Regina, R-E-G-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gaska. G-A-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name is Jerzymowska. J-E-R-Z-Y-M-O-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any nicknames that you were known by back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday-- September the 9th, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now-- I'm going to be 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miedzyrzecz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-E-D-Z-R-Z-Y-- Miedzyrzecz. Something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And is that where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk a bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were six children. Three boys, three girls. And my father worked as a-- in the bristle-- in the-- what do you call it? Toothbrushes-- the making, you know. Very happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- how close were you to your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in there? Were you in the middle? How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was before the youngest. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go into-- your father-- was it a factory he worked in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go in sometimes with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. I did bring him lunch because-- otherwise, we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak inside the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house, we spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spoke Yiddish, Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the city where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews were-- 18,000 Jews in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how many, around how many non-Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. There was the, call it 'Small America.' It was a Jewish town in Miedzyrzecz. It was most Jews. And the farms was Christian. Was very little Christian living in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say religious. It was modern. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We kept tradition. We didn't drive. We didn't drive. We didn't have cars to drive on Saturdays and something. I mean, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did go, most of them on the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about that? About going to synagogue on the holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE]. The flag. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Rosh Hashanah? Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosh Hashanah was very sad in the house because the parents went to the cemetery for praying and things like the that. Otherwise, was happy kids. What did they know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother do a lot of cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Passover like? The Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover was nice. Children came together. They put us to bed before the Seder. We should be up for the Seder. It was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother cook a lot for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a lot of people at the Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't-- well, people-- my brother was married, so he came with his family. And my grandma lived with us, so it was really nice Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grandmother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from my mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Polish schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't bad. It wasn't really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the other children like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boys went to Hebrew school. The girls went to Polish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other children in the school where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very nice. We associate with Polish kids, with friends. We got along nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I remember from my time, no. I don't think so. I don't think so. Maybe I was too silly to understand, or too stupid to understand. We got along nice. That's the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: From school?

speaker: interviewee
text: From school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many, but three of them, I think. We were close. But, otherwise, not too many, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what games you used to like to play? Happy memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like they say, throwing the ball or the gym. Not special. Going around. Joking around. And that's-- were very happy with my friends. I enjoyed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like with so many brothers and sisters? Hard to get attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandma gave a lot of attention to the youngest. I was jealous. That's all. The rest, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get private time with your mother? There were so many other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got along. Mother made always time for us. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you more daddy's girl or mommy's girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. That's nothing matter. I think I loved my mother more. I don't know why. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I loved my mother a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a real happy memory that you could think of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nice. We talked. We joked. The mother had own clique in the neighborhood and the children, we played, jumped rope, jump this, play ball. All kind of things. I played checkers, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the place where you lived? Was it an apartment? Was it a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an apartment, yeah. Second floor, we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share your bedroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: We shared. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many sisters did you share it with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slept with my sister. And my youngest slept with Grandma. My brothers slept two together. And one was out of the house, so it wasn't too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Sabbath like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sabbath was nice, quiet. But in the morning father went to show. Mother went to synagogue too. And then they came home with a light meal. And children run around playing. We played a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too crazy. But I liked school. I had no choice. I had to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything-- did you like to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We liked to read. Yes. Yeah. We compare books with my friends. We share books with my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you liked sports? Did you like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You liked jumping rope. You liked to swim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but like to swim in the summer a lot. I liked jumping the rope. We jumped. And that's all. There's not too much to do. Small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it looked like, the town? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went back eight years ago. It didn't look the same. I couldn't find the street where I was born, was raised. My husband got very upset with me. He said, how could you not remember the street you were born? I said, it doesn't look the same. And I said, why don't you let me go by myself and I'll look around. Maybe I'll find something. Maybe I'll remember something. And I did. I walked by myself and I came to a very narrow street where I used to be afraid to cross at night. And I says, this is the street. And I looked it up. The corner was the same, from both side was buildings. And I said, this street has no name now. It used to be [NON-ENGLISH]. There's no name. You walked in straight and its like an arch. I used to say to my husband we lived in the arch, you know, going inside of the building. And I said, it's a arch, but there was no doors outside. The arch is a fence now. My husband went over and opened the fence. I said, Sam, don't do it. We went inside, I recognized. I recognized the house. Was-- the block was all buildings. It was a narrow street, on both sides was buildings, two-floor buildings, in brick. Balconies. Beautiful. The balconies didn't look the same. They look worse. We went inside. I says I want to go upstairs where I lived. A man came down and started to scream. You cannot go up. It's my house. I build it. And my husband says, we didn't come to buy your house. We didn't come to take your house. We just came. My wife wants me to show where she was born and raised.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a woman came down and we told her, I want to just show my husband the house where I was born. She says, all right. She led us up to the house. I saw things in the house. And I started to shake. And I said, there's another room there. Could I go in? I opened the door to the bedroom. I don't know what I saw there. I took off like a thunder. And I still don't remember what I saw there. And I battle this until now. I touched the chandelier because it was there. I saw Jewish things. We showed the-- we had closet of clothes, you know? They're not like here. They're closets. They're part with a-- I don't know how to explain it-- the closet and two doors was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked at it. The china closet, like here. A china closet is now. But they didn't call it a china closet. But Jewish things like the silver cups, Shabbos candles, and [INAUDIBLE]. All kind of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Sam, I have to get out. I have to go. Like a thunder. The woman offered us tea. I said, I don't want to drink anything. And went downstairs, then Sam says to me, my husband, didn't you say you had a garden? I says, yeah, there was a garden there.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took us out to the garden. The reason they wanted to see the garden because they buried all the silver in the ground. The whole neighborhood buried the silver in the ground from the building.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't there. Everything was moved. I said, Sam, it's not there. It's cleaned out. I said, what is this? She says, this is the toilet. You know what a toilet is. I said, there was no toilet here. The toilet was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady says, yes, that's true. The toilet was there. This is the spot now where the toilet is where they buried the silver. It's now a toilet. It's no silver. And we left. We went to the cemetery. I told my husband, I'd like to see-- my father was buried there. We got to the cemetery. Cemetery was nothing there. [INAUDIBLE] in the cemetery. There's one stone buried from 1946. And the rest was nothing there left. And I couldn't find my father's place. My brothers were there in the cemetery there too in the war, who had got killed. Nothing there. Empty lot. [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's how I left Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start talking now about when the war started in 1939. What do you remember? You were very-- you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you have any memories of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war started with-- well, the Russian came in first, I remember. The Russian came in, and they told us when they come in, they're not going to leave us. They're going to stay until the war is over. Oh, but this was just promises.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was that they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dates, I don't remember anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No dates. And the Russians left. The Germans came. Then the trouble starts. They took out the fixtures, the light, the light bulbs in the houses. Then they have to give up for, then they're to collect for those. They took most of these things, little by little. Liquidate things. You couldn't wear anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took away letter. They took away anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Took a year or something, and they moved us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the ghetto, though, what happened? Did your father lose his business? What are some of the things that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was taken away. You couldn't do. The stores were from the [INAUDIBLE] and the Germans. Everything was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no school. There was no more. Everything stopped with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was day to day life when the Germans first came in before the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took us to work. Clean floors for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, too. yes. Children from ten and older, even younger. We scrubbed floors. And we were scared to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you scrubbed floors and you were scared. Very scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared until they let us back. They took us the peeled potatoes, I remember, something. [INAUDIBLE]. All kind of things. I think in the ghetto they took us out to peel potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before the ghetto, though, what did your mother or father say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't say anything. What do they have to say? My father died in '42, in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how did he die? This wasn't a normal death. It wasn't normal. You know, they did terrible-- what do you call it? They dealt with-- what do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you call it? And they said, they're going to-- who's going to catch him, they'll kill hm, or something like that. They took it very serious. Black market, they did. And he just gave up life. There's no life there. He died in his sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the ghetto, do you remember what the Germans looked like? What color their uniforms were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were green, I think. Light green uniforms, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they on the street all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I was beaten before the ghetto. They checked every house. I don't know what's the reason. The went into every house, checked in. My mother says to me, you can't leave the house alone. I says, I'm the fourth youngest, maybe I'll stay in the house. And I pretend to peel potatoes. When they come in, I'll peel potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I peeled the potatoes, there was a knock on the door. The door wasn't locked. They came in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and looked around. They did find-- I had a very rich uncle-- they find wool. They had a factory for sweaters. So he wanted to protect them, so he gave it all to the family, wool, to save. If you're going to be liberated, we'll have something after the war, because the German liquidate everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got beaten up terrible because the wool was hiding under the mattress in the bed. So everybody thought they shot me, but they shot the man in the attic. Was a man grinding bread. He got shot and they came to the house and saw me alive. They were happy. I said, what happened? I said, I don't know. There was black and blue marks on my back. I couldn't even straighten my back out. And that was-- How old were you? How old you were then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, 10, 12, something. I don't know. Comes to dates, I don't remember anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very hard after your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure it was hard. He was the provider.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before the ghetto in 1941, you were in hiding for awhile, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in German. But Polish people [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you? Why? Did your mother send you into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they said they're going to rape girls. They did rape the girls. They molested them or something, and my mother was afraid of them. They didn't want me sent out to work no more. Because what was going on. Other girl they killed. And this girl got killed. And this one was molested. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you even understand what that meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother explained it to us. Yeah. She says, you're going to stay a couple days till it quiets down. You'll come back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else went? Some of your sisters? Who else went?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My youngest sister stayed with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go into hiding with?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself with the Polish people. My mother knew them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jewish people, these Polish people or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jewish girls were they hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far. Walking distance from their house. Not so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a room? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I slept on the floor in a corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did you remember anything about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's nothing there to remember. I stayed there in the corner until my mother came. She used to deal with them, buy chickens from them, things like that. She says, now, you could come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was attacked when German's dogs, German Shepherds. My mother was attacked, too, when she picked me up. They ripped the clothes off, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: The dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she hurt otherwise, your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was hurt. Yeah. Sure, the dog threw her down and they ripped the clothes off her. And she had-- what do you call it? The jackets? It's not a jackets. They're for the pelts. You know what a pelts is? She had a pelts on, so they ripped it off. They wanted to see what she's carrying underneath.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dogs did this work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were the soldiers with the dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let to your mother go home after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened not far from my house. Not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went back home after your mother picked you up and you went back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I've never been back to this place. No more.

words.text: I've never been back to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it keep-- how did things-- before the ghetto, how did things keep changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changing. Every day, was changing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what sorts of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hiding in the attic. I was hiding under the bed. I was hiding there. It wasn't a normal line.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you know about-- did you know about what was happening in other places?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start hearing about the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto-- my sister wrote us a letter. She was in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw, she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So she wrote us a letter we should move. Maybe times will get better in Russia because she find out it's no food and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we got the letter from my sister. She was in an accident. The Germans attacked and she's in the hospital. And what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came out of the hospital. She was all right. Shop [INAUDIBLE] she got attacked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they did find out she's all right. And we get disconnected. We never knew what happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my older sister, Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a brother who joined the partisans, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he go? What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know what happened. With him, we don't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about when you went to the ghetto. What was that like? How far away was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't too far, the ghetto. And I was-- we stayed in the house with my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, who came with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my youngest sister, and my mother, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this Toby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toby, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The rest wasn't-- everyone was already gone. My brothers wasn't there no more. We lived five family--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the non-Jewish people reacting to all-- everything that was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: They pointed out. When this is-- when they saw a Jew, they'd point it out with a finger She's Jewish. She's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the ghetto, how much were you able to take with you of your belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the clothes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You couldn't. You washed your clothes out at night. And you hang it up in the attic. The next day, they were stolen, you had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you want to the ghetto, you really went with nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: With nothing, yeah. Because you washed something out, somebody stole it from you. And you lived five, six family in one apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the ghetto look like? Was there barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't barbed wire, but there was a gate you couldn't go out. You couldn't go out. You couldn't go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how large the ghetto was? Around how many streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was [INAUDIBLE] streets, yeah. Every time they liquidated some more Jews in the ghetto, and by the end they made Judenrein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the ghetto have a specific name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there more than one ghetto in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived with five families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five, maybe more than five. We had one room. My parents and my sister. And the rest was occupied by other couples.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sleeping arrangements like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't sleeping arrangements. We slept in chairs. We slept on the floor. We slept anyplace we could. But my mother never slept. She's watch us all night. She was like a dog watch, watching that the Germans didn't come. She got gray overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was bad. Was very bod.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you have to stay in line to brush up and get a little water to brush. You have to bring the water in. And it was hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough food? How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they usually did bring in food from someplace. I really don't remember this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how they got the food, so I really don't know. And the ghetto was very bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were starving, dying like flies, covered them with papers in the street.
